video_title: Jojen Paste: does Bran eat Jojen?
claim: The white weirwood paste that Bloodraven and the children of the forest feed Bran to awaken his greenseer powers contains Jojen Reed's blood or body, meaning Bran unknowingly eats Jojen.
sub_claims: Jojen Reed is killed, likely as a blood sacrifice orchestrated by Bloodraven and/or the children of the forest, during Bran's stay in the cave.
Jojen's blood or flesh is what is mixed into the weirwood paste, so when Bran eats the paste he unknowingly commits an act of cannibalism against his friend.
asx_verdict: No direct textual confirmation either way, but the video argues the accumulated circumstantial evidence makes it very plausible, even likely, that Bran unknowingly eats Jojen.
notes: Single theory, book-focused (Bran's ADWD training chapter with Bloodraven is the core scene). One explicit show-divergence aside (the show kills Jojen conventionally with no cannibalism angle) and one explicit self-caveat that there is no direct proof. Argument is built mostly from thematic pattern-matching (magic's price, a recurring cannibalism motif across Bran's arc, Bloodraven's sinister characterization, Bran's disillusionment arc) rather than a single smoking-gun line.

paraphrase: The chapter's tone and imagery are unusually dark and ominous, filled with dead men, dark clouds, blood stew, crows, chambers of bones, a talking corpse, and whispers in the dark, setting the scene for something bad to happen.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter (video: 'this chapter')

paraphrase: The line about the moon being a thin, knife-sharp crescent recurs four times in this chapter, a repeated emphasis on knives, flesh, and blood used to track the passage of time.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter
spoken_quote: The moon was a crescent, thin and sharp as the blade of a knife

paraphrase: The bowl of weirwood-seed paste Bloodraven and the children of the forest give Bran is described as thick and heavy, with dark red veins running through it.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter
spoken_quote: thick and heavy, with dark red veins running through it

paraphrase: The dark red veins running through the paste look remarkably like blood.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: The stated purpose of the paste is to help awaken Bran's gifts and wed him to the trees.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: Bran eats the paste and has a vision through the eyes of a weirwood tree; immediately afterward, he finds Jojen and Meera curiously absent, and that chapter is the last we see of Bran in the books.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: While Bran trains, Jojen mopes around Bloodraven's cave, growing ever more sullen and solitary.
book_hint: Bran POV, cave chapters (exact chapter not specified by the video)

paraphrase: Even though food and rest improve Jojen's physical health after the journey, he seems sadder, sullen, with a weary, haunted look about the eyes.
book_hint: Bran POV, cave chapters (exact chapter not specified by the video)
spoken_quote: sadder now, sullen, with a weary, haunted look about the eyes

paraphrase: At one point Jojen implies that he has reason to be afraid.
Meera says Jojen will not even try to fight his fate, because he says the greendreams do not lie — implying Jojen has resigned himself to something his prophetic dreams have shown him.
book_hint: Meera speaking, cave chapters
spoken_quote: will not even try and fight his fate. He says the greendreams do not lie

paraphrase: Jojen has hinted elsewhere that he has foreseen his own death in his greendreams.
Magic in this world always has a price: when Daenerys uses blood magic to try to save Drogo, the price is a horse's life and her unborn child.
book_hint: Daenerys — the ritual to save Drogo

paraphrase: Melisandre needs king's blood and the burning of innocents to work her most powerful magic.
book_hint: Melisandre's blood magic

paraphrase: Euron Greyjoy makes blood sacrifices to make his ships sail faster, another example of blood magic requiring a price.
book_hint: Euron Greyjoy's blood sacrifices

paraphrase: We are told repeatedly that northmen used to make human sacrifices to the weirwoods, and the Skagosi reportedly still do, supporting the idea that the magic of the old gods specifically requires human sacrifice.

Bran's last vision in the same chapter is of a woman slashing a captive's throat before a weirwood tree, and as the man's life flows out of him in a red tide, Bran can taste the blood — directly juxtaposing the paste-eating scene with old-gods human sacrifice imagery.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: In blood magic, one person's blood can be more powerful than another's (e.g. Melisandre specifically needs king's blood), which could explain why Jojen's blood specifically would be needed: although not a fully-fledged greenseer like Bloodraven, Jojen has green dreams and distinctive moss-green eyes marking his gift, so his blood carries the power of the old gods.
Cannibalism is a recurring theme in the north: Stannis's starving soldiers eat their own fallen.
Wyman Manderley bakes Freys into pies and serves them, another example of cannibalism in the story.
Varamyr eats human flesh while warged into his wolf, another example of cannibalism in the story.

Cannibalism, especially unknowing cannibalism, is specifically a recurring theme in Bran's own chapters: at the Nightfort, Bran recalls the tale of the Rat Cook, who cooked an Andal prince in a pie and served him to his own father, then was cursed to live as a rat eating his own young.
book_hint: Bran POV, the Nightfort

paraphrase: In the cave of the three-eyed crow, Bran and the others eat suspicious meat that might be squirrel, or rat, or maybe something else.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: On the journey north, Coldhands feeds Bran the flesh of some Night's Watchmen and tells him it's pork — an instance of Bran already unknowingly eating human flesh on this same journey.
book_hint: Bran POV, journey north to Bloodraven's cave

paraphrase: Like Varamyr, Bran has already eaten human flesh while warged into his direwolf, establishing that Bran has a precedent of (unknowing) cannibalism.
book_hint: Bran POV, warging chapters

paraphrase: On the journey north, Bran and company eat Coldhands' elk, which Bran feels guilty about because he considers the elk a friend — so Bran has already eaten someone he cared about, not just human flesh in general.
book_hint: Bran POV, journey north

paraphrase: Bloodraven is a hundred-and-twenty-five-year-old figure described as half-corpse and half-tree, undercutting any image of him as a benevolent mentor.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: Weirwood roots are described as snaking in and out of Bloodraven's withered flesh, reinforcing his monstrous, unnatural condition.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter
spoken_quote: in and out of his withered flesh

paraphrase: Bloodraven is described as less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter
spoken_quote: less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool

paraphrase: Bloodraven's cave is littered with the bones of thousands dead, showing his home is a site of mass death.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: Bloodraven tells Bran not to fear the darkness, because darkness will make him strong — dark, seductive, manipulative mentorship rhetoric more like Vader or the Emperor than Yoda.
book_hint: Bloodraven speaking, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter

paraphrase: Bran had expected Bloodraven to be a wise old wizard who could fix his legs; he never signed up to become a psychic tree, underscoring how Bran was misled about what he was getting into.
book_hint: Bran POV, A Dance with Dragons
spoken_quote: a wise old wizard who could fix his legs

paraphrase: Even if Leaf claims Bloodraven works for the greater good, he risks the group's lives bringing them to his cave, never explains what he actually wants, and is turning Bran into something Bran never wanted to become — if sacrificing Jojen served his goals, it's easy to believe he'd do it.

Leaf claims the children of the forest and Bloodraven work for us, for the humans, and for the realms of men — a claimed benevolent motive that the video treats as unproven and possibly self-serving cover.
book_hint: Leaf speaking, A Dance with Dragons — the weirwood-paste/training chapter
spoken_quote: For us, for you, for the realms of men

paraphrase: This parallels Sansa's arc: she dreamed of being a noble lady and got what she wished for, only to discover Joffrey is a monster and Cersei a vile, scheming, evil bitch — a wished-for dream turning out to be horrifying.
book_hint: Sansa POV chapters

paraphrase: Bran is starting to make a similar realization to Sansa's: Coldhands is a monster, and the mystical three-eyed crow he traveled so far to find is actually a creepy half-corpse tree-man — setting up a parallel disillusionment arc that could culminate in the horrific discovery that he ate his friend.
book_hint: Bran POV chapters

paraphrase: Bran has already done messed-up things, like repeatedly warging into Hodor even though it clearly distresses Hodor, suggesting Bran's arc is already trending toward embracing the darkness rather than resisting it.

In the TV show, Jojen is already dead by this point in the story: he is stabbed, then mercy-killed, then blown up by a child of the forest, with no cannibalism or paste element involved — the theory only applies to the book continuity, since the show resolved Jojen's fate differently.
book_hint: Game of Thrones, Season 4

paraphrase: The video explicitly states there is no strong direct evidence that Bran eats Jojen — though it also notes there is no strong evidence against it either.
